title: SEGMENT 8 — Beyond the gut: skin, oral, vaginal, and lung microbiomes

lines.text: SEGMENT 8 — Beyond the gut: skin, oral, vaginal, and lung microbiomes

The gut gets most of the attention, but every body surface that touches the outside world has its own distinctive microbial community, and each of those communities matters for host biology in ways the field is only beginning to map.
Let me give you the highlights for the four other major body-site microbiomes.
The skin microbiome lives on the outer surface of you, which sounds like it should be a single environment but is actually wildly heterogeneous.
Different skin sites have radically different ecologies.

Oily sites — face, scalp, upper back — are dominated by lipophilic Cutibacterium species (formerly called Propionibacterium), particularly Cutibacterium acnes, which feeds on sebum and is the bacterium associated with acne when it overgrows in occluded follicles.
Moist sites — armpits, groin, between toes — are dominated by Staphylococcus and Corynebacterium species, which produce the volatile compounds responsible for body odor.
Dry sites — forearms, lower legs — have lower microbial density and a more even mix of taxa.
Your skin microbiome is shaped by sweat composition, sebum production, pH, immune surveillance, and the relentless mechanical and chemical assault of soap, deodorant, cosmetics, and clothing.
The clinical implications are real but understudied.
Atopic dermatitis — eczema — is associated with reduced skin microbial diversity and overgrowth of Staphylococcus aureus.
Acne is associated with specific strains of C. acnes, not just total bacterial load.
There's growing evidence that early-life skin microbial exposure shapes immune tolerance, with some implications for allergy development.

The frontier is engineered topical microbial products — applying live bacteria to the skin as a treatment for eczema or acne — and a few startups are pursuing this, though the clinical evidence is still early.

The oral microbiome is arguably the most complex microbial community in the human body, with hundreds of bacterial species occupying distinct niches across the tongue, gums, cheek, palate, and tooth surfaces.
The defining feature of the oral environment is biofilms — structured multi-species communities attached to tooth surfaces, called dental plaque.

Plaque is a beautiful and somewhat terrifying example of microbial cooperation: early-colonizing species attach to the tooth surface, modify the local environment, and provide attachment sites for secondary colonizers, building up complex layered communities over hours and days.
Streptococcus mutans and related species ferment dietary sugars to lactic acid, which dissolves tooth enamel — that's the proximate cause of dental caries.

Other species, particularly in subgingival plaque below the gumline, are responsible for periodontal disease — chronic inflammation of the gums that ultimately destroys the tissue holding teeth in place.

Porphyromonas gingivalis is the canonical keystone pathogen of periodontitis, with growing evidence that it also contributes to systemic inflammation and may play a role in cardiovascular disease and even Alzheimer's pathology — though the latter is still contested.
The oral microbiome also matters because the mouth is the entry point for the gut.
Many of the bacteria found at lower abundance in the gut originated in the oral cavity, swallowed in saliva.

And the oral microbiome has become surprisingly important in ancient DNA studies, which we'll come back to in Segment 17 — calcified dental plaque, called dental calculus, traps bacterial DNA from the time of death and preserves it for thousands of years.
The vaginal microbiome is, in healthy women of reproductive age, often dominated by a single genus: Lactobacillus.
This is unusual — most body site microbiomes are diverse — but it's functional.
Lactobacillus species produce lactic acid, which keeps the vaginal pH low (around 4), and this acidic environment suppresses the growth of opportunistic pathogens.

When the Lactobacillus-dominated community is disrupted — by antibiotic use, hormonal changes, douching, or other factors — the result is often bacterial vaginosis, a condition characterized by overgrowth of anaerobic bacteria and increased risk of sexually transmitted infections and preterm birth in pregnancy.

The vaginal microbiome is also a key part of the iHMP phase-two study on pregnancy and preterm birth, which found that specific vaginal community compositions — particularly those low in Lactobacillus crispatus and high in diverse anaerobes — correlate with preterm birth risk, though the causal mechanisms are still being worked out.
The lung microbiome is the most recent body-site microbiome to be widely characterized, and its existence was controversial for a while.
The lungs were classically thought to be sterile, with any bacteria found being interpreted as contamination from the upper airway during sampling.

Sensitive sequencing has now established that healthy lungs do harbor low-density microbial communities, largely seeded by microaspiration of upper airway flora and shaped by host immune surveillance and mucociliary clearance.

The lung microbiome is altered in many diseases — chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, asthma, cystic fibrosis, lung cancer — though again, separating cause from consequence is hard, because diseased lungs have altered immunology and mucus that itself changes microbial colonization.

There are smaller, less-studied microbiomes at other sites — the bladder (yes, urine is not sterile), the breast (with possible implications for breast cancer), even the eye and the inner ear in some recent reports.
Every wet or partially wet surface that interfaces with the outside world has its own community.
The microbiome is not just the gut.
It's a body-wide patchwork of distinctive ecologies, each shaped by local physiology, each potentially relevant to local and systemic health.
